name: Ama-no-Iwato (Heavenly Rock Cave)
shortDescription: Ama-no-Iwato is the cave in Japanese mythology where the sun goddess Amaterasu hid herself, plunging the world into darkness. Her emergence from the cave is a pivotal event in Shinto mythology, restoring light and order to the cosmos.
description: Ama-no-Iwato, translating to "Heavenly Rock Cave," is a significant location in Japanese Shinto mythology. It is the cave where the sun goddess Amaterasu Omikami retreated after being angered by the mischievous behavior of her brother, Susanoo-no-Mikoto. Amaterasu's seclusion within the cave caused the world to be plunged into darkness and chaos, as she is the source of light and warmth. The other kami (gods) gathered to devise a plan to lure her out. They held a boisterous celebration, including Ame-no-Uzume's captivating dance and the creation of a mirror (Yata-no-Kagami) and jewels (Yasakani-no-Magatama). The sounds of merriment and Uzume's suggestive dance piqued Amaterasu's curiosity. When she peeked out of the cave, she saw her reflection in the Yata-no-Kagami and was further enticed by the jewels. The strong god Ame-no-Tajikarao then pulled her completely out of the cave, and the other gods quickly placed a shimenawa rope across the entrance to prevent her from returning, thus restoring light and order to the world. The sakaki tree, often adorned with the mirror and jewels, is used in Shinto rituals to symbolize this event. The cave itself is considered a sacred site, and its story highlights the importance of Amaterasu in Shinto beliefs and the triumph of light over darkness.

location.cosmological: Located in Takamagahara, the heavenly realm of the kami, though earthly locations are associated with the myth, representing a connection between the divine and human worlds.
